data.title: I'm a single parent in my 50s. Should I add my adult children, 23 and 29, to my mortgage loan to help me qualify?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I want to purchase a family home so we three don't have to pay rent'

"We moved around quite a bit over the last few years and had quite a bit of misfortune." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

I am a single parent in my early 50s. A real-estate agent suggested I add my two young-adult children, ages 23 and 29, to a mortgage application. I want to purchase a family home so we three don't have to pay rent, but my income alone is not enough. I know in two to five years they can then either come off the loan or use the equity in the first property to purchase their own homes.

We moved around quite a bit over the last few years and had quite a bit of misfortune, including a few health obstacles, not least COVID, hurricanes in Florida and my going back to school later in life. I was a young teenage parent and my kids went to college in different cities (I moved us as a family). I have known people to first become homeowners when they are older than I am.

I want to do it so we can have a stable existence and won't be moving so much. We'll have a place to make memories and keep all of our personal effects and so forth from over the years. Fifty is not late in life, really. It doesn't have to be a big or expensive home. If I'm paying $2,500 in rent, then a mortgage for approximately $1,700-$2,000 would be better.

The Parent

Don't miss: 'I worked at 14, had an 8.8% mortgage rate and drove used cars': Did boomers really have it easier than millennials?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

You still have a lot of life to live at 50, and it's a good goal to get on the property ladder.

Dear Parent,

Don't take financial advice from a real-estate agent. They have a horse in this race - they want to sell you a home.

It doesn't make them dishonest, it simply means they are not an objective party, and they have their commission rather than your financial welfare front and center. They may wish to see you purchase a home, if that's what you want, but before you put your children on a mortgage, first consult the three "A's" - an adviser, an attorney and/or an accountant.

The short answer is that this move could be good for you, but not so good for your kids. It will tie them into a loan agreement that could affect their ability to take out their own mortgage in the future. If you wanted to take them off the mortgage in the future, you would have to refinance, and that would cost you several thousand dollars.

The short answer is that this move could be good for you, but not so good for your kids.

Because of the persistently high interest rates - the rate for a 30-year mortgage is currently 6.4%, per the latest data from the Mortgage Bankers Association - the housing market has become increasingly out of reach for first-time buyers. Meanwhile, current homeowners have experienced the benefits of a rise in housing equity.

You plan, I assume, to ultimately leave this house to your kids, so they would also benefit from an increase in equity over your lifetime - but if you lose your job and default on the loan, it would be up to them to step in and make the payments. Otherwise, the house would go into foreclosure and all of your credit scores would take an almighty hit.

The idea would be that your kids could, in theory, later find a bank that would approve a loan for them despite this mortgage, given that you would, hopefully, be covering 100% of the payments. Ultimately, however, I'm not a fan of people being on a mortgage but not on a deed. Taking all the risk and none of the ownership leaves the co-signer vulnerable.

Financial skullduggery

Usually, I receive letters from readers whose partners have put their names on the mortgage but not on the deed. That usually involves some kind of legal or logistical skullduggery. This woman discovered her husband's ex-wife had done just that, and he was unable to take himself off the mortgage without the cooperation of the bank and the ex-wife.

This letter writer, meanwhile, said her husband claimed he was afraid she would divorce him if he put her name on the deed of the home he had purchased before they were married (they have been married for three decades). That is a whole new level of financial malfeasance and gaslighting.

In a tale with a different twist, this woman said her estranged husband was on the deed but not on the mortgage. She - surprise, surprise - has been the one paying the mortgage all these years. In her telling, it seems like she was tricked into making that fateful decision. The lesson in all of this: Have an open, frank discussion with your kids.

The age of first-time buyers

Be aware of the different kinds of home ownership. Those also vary by state. Joint tenancy with the right of survivorship gives all owners an equal share of the property and does not allow one owner to add another person to the deed - and, critically, if one of you dies, their share of the property goes to the other owners. Tenants in common, meanwhile, do not have the right of survivorship.

All of this doesn't mean you can't or shouldn't go ahead with this plan at some point. But you should all be aware of the risks and understand that this arrangement would benefit you more than your children. But I fully support your aim to buy a home at 50, and I agree with your reasoning: A stable environment and fixed expenses are a godsend, especially in retirement.

A stable environment and fixed expenses are a godsend, especially in retirement.

You're right about another thing: You still have a lot of life to live at 50, and it's a good goal to get on the property ladder. The median age of first-time home buyers recently reached an all-time high of 38, according to the National Association of Realtors. In the 1980s, first-time home buyers were usually in their late 20s.

"The housing market appears bifurcated between repeat home buyers and first-time home buyers," the NAR report said. "Repeat buyers can enter the housing market with large downpayments (median of 23%), and 31% paid cash and did not finance their home. This is likely due to the increase in housing equity."

"First-time home buyers in the last year shrunk to an historic low of just 24% of all buyers," it added. "Prior to 2008, the share of first-time buyers had a historical norm of 40%. In the last two years, first-time home buyer household income has grown by $26,000. This year's report shows that the median household income of first-time home buyers was $97,000."

Whatever you do, don't give up on your dream of homeownership.

Related: 'My retirement is going to be a disaster': I'm 59 and have $45,000 in my 401(k). I earn $72,000. Am I doomed?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'We are acquaintances, not friends': My neighbors need somewhere to stay after a house fire. Do I say yes?

'The sky has not fallen': Is it finally time to stop worrying about a recession?

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1115ET

data.title: Global Plant Nutrition Company Launches Trial Program to Expand Access of Sustainable Growing Solutions
data.description:
Global Plant Nutrition Company Launches Trial Program to Expand Access of Sustainable Growing Solutions

International Cultivation Experts CANNA Provide Chance to Test Compostable Propagation Solutions Designed for Healthy Early Growth

CANNA, global experts in plant nutrition and substrate synergy, announced today the launch of a trial application for qualified licensed growers to experience CANNA Rootplugs — a propagation solution engineered for optimal seed starting and cutting success. The rootplugs are designed for rooting, are fully compostable and degradable, and provide the ideal grow environment that can help reduce pre-veg time.

“CANNA is proud to provide our new Rootplugs for our loyal customers including commercial growers, breeders and home gardeners,” said Abha Gupta M.S., lead horticulturist at CANNA USA. “They are a sustainable and versatile option for all types of cultivation, and since they are packaged dry, their shelf life is extended and the risk of fungal contamination reduced.”

CANNA Rootplugs are designed to support root development and are suitable for all seeds and cuttings. Unlike other root plugs, CANNA plugs break down easily and compost into the growing medium after transplanting. They offer quick water absorption and the unique structure maintains a desirable balance between aeration and moisture retention to support rapid rooting.

Composed of 10% European peat and 90% coco coir, CANNA Rootplugs seamlessly integrate with natural substrates. As with other CANNA products, they are produced with a focus on quality, sterility and sustainability. CANNA Rootplugs are available in 1,000 units per box.

About CANNA

CANNA is a global leader in plant cultivation solutions specializing in the production of base nutrients, additives, and substrates. With a passion for plants and a commitment to helping growers achieve optimal results, CANNA offers high-quality, environmentally friendly products tailored to every growing medium. From research and development to production and logistics, CANNA oversees the entire process to ensure consistent quality. CANNA is trusted in over 30 countries and empowers growers—novice and expert alike—with innovative solutions that make cultivation clean, simple and sustainable. Learn more: cannagardening.com

Media Contact
Erin Curry
Grasslands: A Journalism-Minded Agency
erin@mygrasslands.com

data.title: Cipher Mining Shares Fall After Disclosing Senior Notes Offering
data.description:
By Freddy Sebastian 




  Shares of Cipher Mining slid after it disclosed a private senior notes offering of $800 million. 



  Shares of the bitcoin miner recently fell 4.9% to $13.48. 



  Cipher said it expects to grant the initial purchasers of the notes a 13-day option to purchase up to an additional $120 million of notes. 



  The convertible senior notes will mature on Oct. 1, 2031, unless earlier repurchased, redeemed or converted, the company said. 



  In connection with the pricing of the notes, Cipher expects to enter into privately-negotiated capped call transactions with one or more of the initial purchasers, affiliates or other financial institutions. 



  The company said it plans to use part of the proceeds from the offering to fund the cost of the capped call transactions and the remainder to finance a portion of data center construction, accelerate the build-out of a high-performance computing strategy across its 2.4 GW pipeline and to continue to expand its pipeline of development sites. 





  Write to Freddy Sebastian at freddy.sebastian@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 25, 2025 11:06 ET (15:06 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Tri Pointe Homes Unveils Stylish Homes at McCormick Trails, A Kitsap Peninsula Community Surrounded by Trees
data.description:
Tri Pointe Homes Unveils Stylish Homes at McCormick Trails, A Kitsap Peninsula Community Surrounded by Trees

30-home community in Port Orchard, Wash. Offers “Hero” Incentives for active military, veterans

Tri Pointe Homes® (NYSE: TPH), one of the nation’s largest homebuilders and a recognized leader in innovative design, customer experience, and sustainability, is slated to begin sales Sept. 27 at its brand-new McCormick Trails community in Port Orchard.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250925122272/en/

McCormick Trails, Tri Pointe Homes' new community in Port Orchard, Wash., blends modern living with the natural beauty of the Pacific Northwest. Photo credit: Tri Pointe Homes

Tri Pointe is introducing 30 thoughtfully designed single-family homes at McCormick Trails, a tucked-away neighborhood bordered by 21 acres of protected natural space that gives many homes beautiful exposure to the surrounding forest. Located at 7311 SW Kerbin Ln, this new enclave blends modern living with the natural beauty of the Pacific Northwest.

Homes at McCormick Trails range from 2,809 to 3,311 square feet, with four to five bedrooms, 2.5 to 3.5 bathrooms, and two- to four-bay garages. Homes are anticipated to start from the $700ks. Flexible layouts offer main floor living with home offices or studies on the main floor plus guest suites with full baths. Farmhouse-inspired exteriors in moss and neutral tones complement the natural surroundings, while covered patios and mud rooms make for easy year-round indoor-outdoor living.

Each home comes equipped with the latest HomeSmart® technology, including a video doorbell for security, a WiFi door lock controlling access to the home, and the ability to control temperature, lighting, and more via connected devices. Thoughtful design facilitates space for virtually everything homeowners need and love to do. Interiors come standard with abundant storage, including walk-in pantries, extra closets, and lofts, along with two- to four-bay garages to make room for skis, bikes, kayaks, and other hobbies.

Active-duty military members and veterans can receive up to $5,000 in closing credit as a “hero” incentive towards home personalization options from Tri Pointe’s Design Studio when financing through Tri Pointe Connect®. At Tri Pointe’s state-of-the-art Design Studio, buyers can bring their vision to life by choosing from a wide array of finishes and details, from flooring to fixtures, creating a home that truly reflects their lifestyle.

“These homes are designed for everyday Northwest living, with flexible spaces, inviting patios, and nature right outside your door,” said Tri Pointe Washington’s VP of Community Experience Tricia Lynn. “Paired with miles of trails and McCormick Village Park, it’s a place where neighbors can interact and everyone can thrive.”

Connected by more than two miles of pedestrian and bike trails, McCormick Trails links seamlessly to the broader McCormick community, where residents can wander over eight miles of forested hiking paths and soak in the true Pacific Northwest experience. Residents also enjoy access to the expansive new 65-acre McCormick Village Park with fenced dog parks and a splash pad, as well as a golf course and clubhouse, pickleball and basketball courts. McCormick Village’s new retail center, offering shops, dining, and services, is anticipated to open in 2026. Within the McCormick Trails neighborhood itself, an additional two-acre park will provide a children’s play area and pickleball court.

To join the McCormick Trails interest list, visit https://www.tripointehomes.com/wa/greater-seattle-area/mccormick-trails/.

About Tri Pointe Homes®

One of the largest homebuilders in the U.S., Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (NYSE: TPH) is a publicly traded company operating in 12 states and the District of Columbia, and is a recognized leader in customer experience, innovative design, and environmentally responsible business practices. The company builds premium homes and communities with deep ties to the communities it serves—some for as long as a century. Tri Pointe Homes combines the financial resources, technology platforms and proven leadership of a national organization with the regional insights, longstanding community connections and agility of empowered local teams. Tri Pointe has won multiple Builder of the Year awards and was named 2024 Developer of the Year. The company is one of the 2023 and 2025 Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For® and was designated as one of the PEOPLE Companies That Care® for three consecutive years (2023 through 2025). The company was also named as a Great Place To WorkCertified™ company for five years in a row (2021 through 2025), and was named on several Great Place To Work® Best Workplaces list (2022 through 2025). Building upon the legacy that was established more than 50 years ago under the name Quadrant Homes®, Tri Pointe Homes is an award-winning leader in the Washington real estate sector. For more information, please visit TriPointeHomes.com.

Lisa Graham, Tri Pointe Homes
Lisa.graham@tripointehomes.com; 425-301-9082

May Wildman, C+C for Tri Pointe Homes
Mwildman@cplusc.com; 702-301-8496

data.title: Center for Disaster Philanthropy Announces $6.8M in New Grants from Truist Foundation-Supported Fund for Ongoing Helene Recovery Efforts in Western North Carolina
data.description:
In January 2025, the Center for Disaster Philanthropy and Truist Foundation announced the creation of the Western North Carolina Recovery and Resiliency Fund with $21M to support medium- and long-term needs

WASHINGTON, DC / ACCESS Newswire / September 25, 2025 / The Center for Disaster Philanthropy (CDP), a nonprofit organization that mobilizes philanthropy to support recovery from disasters, today announced $6.8 million in funding for community-led recovery efforts as part of the second round of grants from its Truist Foundation Western North Carolina Recovery and Resiliency Fund to support ongoing recovery efforts following Hurricane Helene. This investment in recovery is part of Truist and Truist Foundation's $725 million Truist Cares for Western North Carolina initiative.

"We know that when local communities and organizations are at the center of efforts to rebuild, leading the way with their knowledge and dedication, long-term recovery is more sustainable and effective," said Patricia McIlreavy, President and CEO of CDP. "Thanks to Truist Foundation's support, we are proud to stand alongside the people and organizations of Western North Carolina as recovery from Hurricane Helene continues. The strength and creativity of local leaders inspire us, and we are committed to supporting their vision for a recovery that includes everyone. Together, we can help ensure that every community has the resources and support it needs to rebuild and thrive."

In September 2024, Hurricane Helene devastated Western North Carolina with damaging winds, unprecedented levels of flooding and landslides. The storm killed 108 people, including 100 from Western North Carolina, and destroyed dozens of homes, businesses, and other critical infrastructure in the area. A year later, the community continues to rebuild and experience the lasting impacts of Helene's devastation, even as several encouraging recovery efforts are underway.

In January 2025, Truist Foundation announced the launch of CDP's Truist Foundation Western North Carolina Recovery and Resiliency Fund, a partnership that is part of a $725 million commitment from Truist and Truist Foundation called Truist Cares for Western North Carolina. Through the Fund, CDP addresses medium- and long-term needs to help strengthen the region's housing and small businesses. In June 2025, CDP announced that the first $3.3 million in grants had been awarded to four organizations actively leading recovery efforts.

"At Truist Foundation, we believe that community investment is most impactful when it's rooted in the voices and leadership of local communities," said Lynette Bell, president of Truist Foundation. "Guided by Truist's purpose to inspire and build better lives and communities, we are proud to partner with the Center for Disaster Philanthropy to support organizations that are driving long-term recovery across the region. This second round of grants reflects our continued commitment to helping Western North Carolina rebuild in ways that are inclusive, adaptive and forward-looking."

CDP's grantmaking is guided by its assessment of damages, systemic marginalization, community capacity and unmet needs to support the area's equitable recovery.

Learn more about the Round 2 grantees below:

  • Appalachian Community Capital Development Foundation was awarded $1 million to advance small business recovery and resilience in Western North Carolina through their connection to community development financial institutions (CFDIs) and direct small business grant opportunities. Funds will also support technical assistance and organizational infrastructure development to sustain operations and strengthen planning around future disasters.

  • Boone Area Chamber of Commerce Economic Development Commission Foundation received $250,000 for long-term economic recovery and small business resilience in Ashe, Avery and Watauga Counties. The funding will support direct subgrants and recovery training programs for small businesses.

  • Carolina Farm Stewardship Association received $200,000 to support independent farmers through its Western North Carolina Food System Recovery and Resilience Initiative. Funding will support small business grant opportunities, technical assistance and tools and resources for farms impacted by Helene.

  • Children and Families Resource Center received $100,000 to support long-term housing recovery and supportive services through their disaster case navigation program in Henderson County.

  • Colaborativa La Milpa, as a fiscal sponsor for PODER Emma, received $500,000 for long-term housing recovery in the Carolina Wren Housing Cooperative in the unincorporated Buncombe County community of Emma. The funding will support mobile home replacement and refurbishment, first-time homebuyer education and economic recovery initiatives.

  • High Country Caregiver Foundation received $350,000 to support housing repair and replacement in Avery, Mitchell and Yancey Counties. The funding will support dozens of families enrolled in the organization's kinship care case management system by repairing heavily impacted homes, including mobile homes. The initiative aims to stabilize housing to allow families to remain together and more fully participate in the organization's support programs.

  • Madison Alliance for Rebuilding Communities, through fiscal sponsor Community Housing Coalition of Madison County, received $488,625 for its Rebuild and Fortification Program. The funding will support direct repairs, rebuilds and mitigation, along with associated case management services, in rural Madison County communities.

  • Mennonite Disaster Service was awarded $300,000 to support the renovation of a church into a dormitory to house cohorts of up to 40 skilled volunteers who will deploy throughout the impacted region to repair and rebuild homes and private-access bridges.

  • Mitchell County Development Foundation received $510,000 to advance small business recovery. The funding will support small business grant opportunities in Spruce Pine and throughout the county to allow businesses to reopen or sustain operations.

  • MountainTrue was awarded $750,000 to support waterway cleanup in Helene-impacted waterways. The initiatives will support small businesses that drive the outdoor recreation economy, monitor and help restore river water quality, and enhance resiliency.

  • The National Community Pharmacists Association Foundation received $300,000 to support independent community pharmacies throughout Western North Carolina as they recover from Helene. This funding will support equipment, technical assistance, certification and access to workforce development resources that will increase community resilience.

  • Northwestern Housing Enterprises, Inc., also known as Northwestern Regional Housing Authority (NWRHA), received $750,000 for long-term housing recovery. Funding will support the development of two affordable-housing communities in Yancey and Avery Counties.

  • Pisgah Legal Services received $400,000 to provide no-cost disaster-related legal services to survivors and eligible disaster-impacted nonprofit organizations across 18 counties in Western North Carolina.

  • Swannanoa Communities Together, via fiscal sponsor Asheville Creative Arts, Inc., received $300,000 for housing recovery, case navigation services and community-led disaster recovery initiatives for the unincorporated Swannanoa Valley.

  • Transylvania Habitat for Humanity received $585,000 for housing recovery and case navigation services in Transylvania County. The funding will support sustainable long-term housing by delivering disaster-resilient housing solutions and reinforcing community infrastructure.

CDP funds a wide range of critical programs in communities devastated by wildfires, hurricanes, flooding and other natural hazards, as well as humanitarian crises. These efforts include mental health initiatives, housing repair, food access and other essential services. CDP also offers free educational resources and advises corporations, foundations and individual donors to ensure that they can more effectively support equitable, community-led disaster preparedness and recovery.

# # #

About the Center for Disaster Philanthropy

CDP mobilizes philanthropy to strengthen communities' ability to withstand disasters and recover equitably when they occur. It provides expert advice and educational resources, supports diverse coalitions and manages domestic and international disaster funds on behalf of corporations, foundations and individuals through targeted, holistic and localized grantmaking. Find out more at disasterphilanthropy.org and on X and LinkedIn.

About Truist Foundation

Truist Foundation is committed to Truist Financial Corporation's (NYSE: TFC) purpose to inspire and build better lives and communities. The foundation, an endowed private foundation established in 2020 whose operating budget is independent of Truist Financial Corporation, makes strategic investments in a wide variety of nonprofit organizations centered around two focus areas: building career pathways to economic mobility and strengthening small businesses to ensure all communities have an equal opportunity to thrive. Embodying these focus areas are the foundation's leading initiatives - the Inspire Awards and Where It Starts. Learn more at Truist.com/Foundation.

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Truist on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:
Spokesperson: Truist
Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/truist
Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Truist



View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Canada Watchdog Projects Wider Deficits in Near Term Amid Tariff-Fueled Struggles — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira 




  OTTAWA--Canada's budget deficit is projected to widen and surpass 2% of the economy for the current and upcoming fiscal years due to weak growth and spending measures planned by Prime Minister Mark Carney to rewire the economy, according to the country's fiscal watchdog. 



  The Office of the Parliamentary Budget Officer said its forecast is based on spending measures unveiled up to Sept. 5, or the day that Carney rolled out billions of dollars of aid to industries facing the biggest squeeze from President Trump's tariffs. The Liberal government is expected to unveil additional spending in a budget plan set for release Nov. 4, so what the budget watchdog is presenting could be subject to upward revisions. 



  The budget watchdog anticipates the budget deficit for the current 2025-26 fiscal year, ending March 31, 2026, will balloon to 68.5 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of $49.3 billion. The annual shortfall represents 2.2% of gross domestic product. The budget watchdog projects another hefty deficit in the 2026-27 fiscal year, C$64.2 billion, or 2% of GDP. 



  Canadian officials have warned of a "substantial" budget deficit for the current fiscal year, as the government rapidly ramps up defense spending and deploys C$25 billion toward nation-building projects like cross-country trade corridors. Furthermore, the budget watchdog's forecast is not as stark as those presented by other economists. The C.D. Howe Institute think tank, with help from former TD Bank chief economist and senior Canadian official Don Drummond, has warned of a C$90 billion budget deficit this fiscal year. 



  Carney said the Nov. 4 budget would incorporate austerity measures as the government tries to reduce spending on government operations to free up cash for infrastructure projects. Those austerity measures could mean job losses in the federal public sector, the country's finance minister has suggested. A much-wider budget deficit, closer to C$100 billion, would "face unusually tough scrutiny from investors," said Jimmy Jean, chief economist at Desjardins Group. He said the verdict from bond investors likely hinges on how viable the government's plans are to reduce program spending, and quickly build infrastructure projects that Carney has deemed crucial for revamping the economy. 



  The fiscal deterioration is the result of the Canadian economy being caught in a turbulent period, as the hit from Trump's hefty tariffs fuels job losses in trade-exposed sectors and beyond. The economy contracted in the second quarter, 1.6% annualized, and joblessness has climbed to its highest level in nine years, excluding the Covid-19 period. 



  The Bank of Canada cut its main interest rate last week, to 2.5%, and warned of slow growth for the foreseeable future. Businesses have either scaled back or halted investment and hiring plans. Inflation-adjusted spending on machinery and equipments reached its lowest level since the 1980s in the second quarter, and job vacancies sit at a seven-year low. 



  The budget watchdog's forecast assumes that the central bank's main interest rate remains unchanged until late next year, when it climbs to 2.75%. 



  The parliamentary budget office said it downgraded the level of nominal GDP -- or the broadest measure of the government's tax base -- by about C$13 billion a year from now to 2029, reflecting the impact of U.S. tariffs. The watchdog also downgraded its forecast for real GDP, and expects expansion of 1.2% this year and 1.3% in 2026. 





  Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com 


 
 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 25, 2025 10:56 ET (14:56 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Home sales stall, even as housing market becomes buyer-friendly
data.description:
By Aarthi Swaminathan

Affordability remains constrained across the nation

The median price of an existing home in August was $422,600, according to the National Association of Realtors.

After 14 months of house hunting, Tyesha Hall finally bought her first home.

Hall, 39, who works at a nonprofit, had been looking to buy a house in Detroit because she plans to expand her family. It was also a personal goal for her to be the first person in her immediate family to own a home.

"For the last three years, I was really working on my credit score. I went from somewhere around 515 and ended up at 821," Hall told MarketWatch. She meticulously saved and watched her spending, which included curtailing going out with friends.

Recent home buyer Tyesha Hall, left, and real-estate agent Shaunise Williams, right, worked to get Hall her dream home.

After getting her finances in good shape, she budgeted a maximum of $190,000 for a single-family home and contacted her real-estate agent, Shaunise Williams with eXp Realty. After looking at nearly 20 homes with Williams, Hall decided to make an offer on a place she thought was perfect.

She soon hit a roadblock. Hall was outbid by a rival buyer who was offering all cash. She decided to move on. But before she even started looking at other houses, that listing agent called Williams. Was Hall still interested in the house? The cash offer had fallen through.

Hall bought the home in early September for $155,000. The purchase made her the first homeowner among her seven siblings.

"I feel overwhelmingly happy," she said. "Because I'm the oldest of eight, seeing my sisters and brothers come to me and tell me how proud they are of me, it makes everything worth it. I'm extremely happy and extremely blessed."

Hall's story had a happy ending, but her months-long effort to buy a home highlights a big challenge in real estate. Even though many parts of the nation have become a lot more buyer-friendly, buyers are not necessarily purchasing homes with ease, and they are still dealing with competition.

"It's only a buyer's market for those who can afford to buy," economists at real-estate brokerage Redfin (RKT) said in a blog post.

Hall was able to cover part of her purchase with $40,000 in grants from local financial institutions because she was a first-time buyer as well as the first in her generation of her family to own a home.

Multiple states, including Michigan, offer down-payment assistance to first-generation home buyers. The Michigan program offers up to $25,000 to buyers to cover down-payment and closing costs.

Hall closed on the 1,100 square-foot, three-bedroom home in early September. Her 30-year mortgage rate was 6.2%. She plans to refinance the loan if rates come down enough.

The real-estate market in metropolitan areas in the Midwest and the Northeast is relatively competitive, even as the national housing market remains mired in a slowdown.

"We're not seeing any breakouts," Lawrence Yun, chief economist at the National Association of Realtors, said in a press call on Thursday. Sales remain sluggish for now and "pretty much stuck at historically low levels," he added.

Sales of existing homes - that is, homes that are not brand new -remained depressed in August as most buyers stayed out of the market. The market that month was still very expensive, as mortgage rates remained elevated on top of high home prices. The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage averaged 6.37% as of Sept. 24, according to Mortgage News Daily.

But sales in certain regions have been relatively brisk. Sales picked up the most in the Midwest, the most affordable region in the nation as of August.

"The Midwest region is the outperformer in terms of sales activity as well as the price," Yun said.

"Part of the reason is affordability conditions. The Midwest is much more affordable compared to the rest of the country," he added. Hence, "it is performing better in terms of sales," and home prices were still appreciating while the rest of the nation has seen a slowdown.

Existing-home sales fall in August

Sales of existing homes fell nationwide by 0.2% in August from the previous month, according to the NAR, dropping to a 4 million pace.

That's the number of homes that would be sold over an entire year if sales took place at the same rate in every month as they did in August. The numbers are seasonally adjusted.

Home sales were slightly elevated compared with the same month a year ago.

The pace of sales exceeded expectations of economists surveyed by Dow Jones Newswires and the Wall Street Journal, who expected home sales to fall to a 3.96 million pace in August.

Home sales in the Midwest pick up

Home sales picked up in the Midwest and the West, while the rest of the nation was still seeing lackluster demand.

In the Midwest, home sales grew 2.1% in August from the previous month. The median price of a home sold in the region was $330,500. Prices also rose 4.5% from last August.

Even so, the median home price in the Midwest was 22% below the national median sales price, Yun noted, which was part of the reason the region performed better than others.

Sales grew to a smaller extent in the West, up by 1.4% from the previous month. The median sales price in the region was much higher, at $624,300. Prices only grew 0.6% from last August.

Home prices inch up

The median price for an existing home in August was $422,600, the NAR said.

Home prices increased 2% from one year ago. Nationally, 20% percent of homes were sold above list price, which was unchanged from a year ago. Homes received an average of 2.5 offers.

Homes priced at $1 million and above were selling the most. In August, sales of such homes rose 8.2% from a year ago, likely because of the run-up in stock prices and housing wealth, Yun said. Many buyers of such homes are moving up from a less expensive home, rather than first-time buyers.

Homes priced from $100,000 to $250,000 - the category Hall's Detroit home fell into - slumped 1.2% from last August.

Homes stay on the market for longer

The NAR data also showed that listed homes remained on the market for 31 days on average, which was a lot slower than last August. Last year, homes sold in 26 days, on average.

Even though it's taking longer for homeowners to sell their homes, some are no longer cutting prices to attract buyers.

In August, the share of for-sale listings that had their asking price reduced was 26%, according to data from real-estate platform Zillow. That was down from 27% in July.

Price cuts were most common in Salt Lake City, Denver and Raleigh, N.C.

They were least common in Boston, Milwaukee and New York City.

Housing inventory rises across the U.S.

The total number of homes listed for sale in August rose 11.7% from last year, to 1.53 million units. There is a 4.6-month supply of unsold inventory, which is considered by the industry to be a balanced level.

All-cash buyers made up 28% of sales, which was slightly elevated.

The share of individual investors or second-home buyers was 21%, and 28% of buyers were first-timers.

-Aarthi Swaminathan

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-25-25 1032ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan Inc. Investigated for Securities Fraud Violations - Contact the DJS Law Group to Discuss Your Rights – FCX
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan Inc. Investigated for Securities Fraud Violations - Contact the DJS Law Group to Discuss Your Rights – FCX

The DJS Law Group announces that it is investigating claims on behalf of investors of Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (“Freeport” or “the Company”) (NYSE: FCX) for violations of the securities laws.

INVESTIGATION DETAILS: The investigation focuses on whether the Company issued misleading statements and/or failed to disclose information pertinent to investors. Reuters reported on September 24, 2025, that Freeport “declared force majeure at its Grasberg mine in Indonesia and said it is expecting consolidated sales to be lower for copper and gold in the third quarter.” The Company had previously halted production at the mine after a massive flow of wet material had blocked portions of the mine, trapping miners. At least two workers were fatally injured in the event. Freeport shares fell sharply in response to this news.

If you are a shareholder who suffered a loss, contact us to participate.

WHY DJS LAW GROUP? DJS Law Group’s primary focus is to enhance investor return through balanced counseling and aggressive advocacy. We specialize in securities class actions, corporate governance litigation, and domestic/international M&A appraisals. Our clients are some of the largest and most sophisticated hedge funds and alternative asset managers in the world. The litigation claims of our clients are extraordinarily valuable assets that demand respect, focus, and results.

This press release may be considered Attorney Advertising in some jurisdictions under the applicable law and rules of ethics.

David J. Schwartz
DJS Law Group
274 White Plains Road, Suite 1
Eastchester, NY 10709
Phone: 914-206-9742
Email: David@djslawllp.com

data.title: eClinical Solutions Explores Data Intelligence in Modern Clinical Trials at ENGAGE 2025
data.description:
eClinical Solutions Explores Data Intelligence in Modern Clinical Trials at ENGAGE 2025

Two-day event congregates life science leaders to push boundaries, embrace data technology innovation, and achieve the extraordinary in drug development

eClinical Solutions LLC, a global provider of digital clinical software and services, will bring together global technology and biopharma leaders from October 29-30 for its seventh annual client and partner conference, ENGAGE 2025. The company will host over 200 attendees at The Bellevue Hotel in Philadelphia, PA, including representatives from over 75 life science organizations such as Bristol Myers Squibb, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Immunovant, and Kailera Therapeutics, and industry partner corporate sponsors including PwC.

As cycle time becomes the new currency of drug development, life sciences R&D leaders are looking to expedite adoption of AI and risk management for greater efficiency across the clinical trial lifecycle. As such, this year’s agenda focuses on the latest advancements in AI-driven automation, agentic AI, and risk-based quality management (RBQM) to accelerate clinical trials.

“According to data reported by clinical trial leaders in the annual Industry Outlook Survey, organizations are doubling down on AI adoption, with 72% of respondents already having integrated AI across multiple workflows or actively exploring AI,” said Raj Indupuri, CEO and co-founder of eClinical Solutions. “The life sciences industry is looking for best practices and hands-on guidance for tech foundations and data strategies that support AI adoption and speed timelines. That’s why ENGAGE focuses on strategically and tactically exploring how modern data stacks reduce fragmentation for frictionless data flows in support of AI-automation, holistic risk management, and agentic AI.”

With the focus on acceleration and driving innovation, this year’s keynote speaker will be former Formula 1 and Indy 500 driver, international broadcaster, and author, Derek Daly. Daly will draw from his experience in the high-stakes world of professional motorsports, including his 17-year professional racing career, to share lessons in collaborating to become fast and pushing boundaries to attain breakthroughs.

Key themes and insights to look forward to at this year’s ENGAGE conference include:

AI and Automation
Rich Gleeson of Kailera Therapeutics shares the foundational role of seamless data integration in enabling AI-driven trials, illustrating how connected systems are essential for unlocking the full potential of this new technology. To that end, Nathan Johnson, VP, product management at eClinical Solutions will highlight new agentic AI and automation-driven mapping to eliminate time-intensive steps for data standardization and submission. Continuing the momentum, Jazz Pharmaceuticals’ Alex Crawford demonstrates how AI is set to revolutionize audit trail reviews, driving both efficiency and compliance. While Ian Shafer from PwC is set to highlight the power and necessity of process automation to reduce cycle times and bring therapies to market more efficiently. These engaging sessions will demonstrate real-world applications driving today’s rapid evolution of intelligent, tech-enabled clinical operations that scale clinical trial innovation.

Risk Management Strategies
Robin Rimsky of Forza Clinical presents a holistic review of RBQM strategies, while Alice Phung from Bristol Myers Squibb dives into a real-world protocol deviations use case, demonstrating risk mitigation in action and offering tangible takeaways for sponsors and sites alike. eClinical Solutions’ Nathan Johnson connects the dots between risk assessment and data review, offering a forward-looking view into how the AI-powered elluminate Clinical Data Cloud® is designed to support integrated risk and quality management in a single platform. Outside of traditional sessions, a dedicated RBQM roundtable will bring trial experts together to share their insights and discuss how organizations can adapt to the industry shift away from RBQM as a point solution toward risk mitigation fully integrated across the clinical trial system. Another panel session, “Data Management Industry Outlook,” will include Immunovant and Biogen leaders discussing practical ways data management, biostatistics, and programming can prepare for the future of clinical data and the evolution to risk-based data management (RBDM).

Data Intelligence
eClinical’s co-founder and CEO, Raj Indupuri, shares his vision on how the new data stack for modern clinical trials yields measurable value across time, systems, and resources and prepares organizations for the future. In parallel, Salam Ammus of BMS will share a case study on results of a system and data consolidation initiative. Jonathan Rowe and Andrew Johnston of ZS will echo this sentiment in session spotlights on portfolio-level RBQM and digitizing Integrated Data Review Plan (IDRP) capabilities to leverage centralized data for quality and efficiency at scale. Innovation and lessons convene at the ‘Change Makers Showcase,’ featuring argenx, Genmab, and Vertwo Advisory on their strategies for data innovation, including advice for change management that drives ROI from clinical data stack modernization.

For more information about ENGAGE and to register, please visit: www.eclinicalsol.com/events/engage/.

About eClinical Solutions
eClinical Solutions’ industry-leading data & analytics platform, elluminate®, and biometrics services experts help biopharma researchers at large, mid-size, and emerging life sciences organizations develop tomorrow’s breakthroughs with today’s resources. eClinical is a privately-held, purpose-driven company with a global workforce and winner of two 2025 Top Workplaces Culture Excellence Awards, the 2024 Top Workplaces USA Award, and Great Place To Work® India Certification™. Learn more at www.eclinicalsol.com and follow eClinical Solutions on LinkedIn.

Olivia Mannion
eClinical@pancomm.com

data.title: TWOPAGES Unveils 2025 Autumn/Winter Home Trends, Blending Texture, Heritage, and Functionality
data.description:
TWOPAGES Unveils 2025 Autumn/Winter Home Trends, Blending Texture, Heritage, and Functionality

TWOPAGES Unveils 2025 Autumn/Winter Home Trends, Blending Texture, Heritage, and Functionality

PR Newswire

SPOKANE, Wash., Sept. 25, 2025

SPOKANE, Wash., Sept. 25, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- As the demand for elevated living spaces continues to grow, TWOPAGES Curtains, a provider of high-quality, stylish custom window treatments, has introduced its 2025 Autumn/Winter interior trends, setting the tone for the season with three guiding themes: Warm Minimalism × Layered Neutrals, Heritage Pattern × Modern Collaboration, and Luxe Comfort × Functional Elegance.

The first trend, Warm Minimalism × Layered Neutrals, embraces earthy tones and natural materials to create interiors that feel restorative yet sophisticated. The Jawara Luxury Linen Cotton Curtain embodies this spirit, offering a heavyweight linen-cotton blend with a refined slubbed texture. Its breathable, eco-conscious composition makes it as functional as it is aesthetic. The Linnea Linen Cotton Embroidered Curtain takes this further, weaving delicate leaf motifs across soft fabric to add subtle dimensions to neutral interiors. For those seeking warmth and substance, the Karin Pure Wool Curtain provides natural insulation, balancing temperature while enhancing privacy with its dense, midweight wool. Completing this grouping, the Veda Luster Cotton Velour Drapery brings a dramatic touch through its high-shine velour finish, transforming everyday spaces into refined sanctuaries.

While minimalism speaks to calm and comfort, TWOPAGES also explores the power of heritage. The second theme, Heritage Pattern × Modern Collaboration, draws on classic motifs and partnerships with contemporary designers to bring timeless patterns into today's homes. The Sara Le x TWOPAGES collection illustrates how earthy tones and neutral palettes can achieve quiet sophistication, while remaining approachable in design and function. Similarly, Canadian designer Britt Bengoechea collaborates with TWOPAGES on a floral collection inspired by European countryside aesthetics. These patterns, reinterpreted with modern sensibility, bring depth and warmth without overwhelming contemporary interiors. Among standalone designs, the Holly Country Floral Drapery pleated curtain adds bohemian character with a curated mix of artistic floral prints, offering both softness and practicality. The Celia Striped Plaid Collection introduces a casual, rustic note, balancing timeless stripes and plaids with everyday durability. Collectively, these designs demonstrate how tradition and modernity can be layered to create homes that feel familiar yet fresh.

The third trend, Luxe Comfort × Functional Elegance, responds to a growing preference for fabrics that are both indulgent and highly practical. Velvet takes center stage here, serving as both a visual and tactile highlight. The Birkin Velvet Curtain exemplifies this with plush polyester velvet that cascades in structured folds, delivering both dramatic presence and functional benefits such as light blocking and thermal insulation. Expanding on this idea, the Amy Switzer x TWOPAGES collaboration reimagines velvet through a contemporary lens, introducing deep jewel tones, painterly motifs, and ombré layering. This collection highlights how velvet can shift from vintage charm to modern artistry without losing its sense of luxury. For interiors requiring heightened performance, the Tavis Premium Velvet Curtain combines dense velvet with exceptional sound-dampening and insulating properties, making it as technically advanced as it is visually appealing.

Taken together, these three design directions highlight TWOPAGES's ongoing exploration of how materials, craftsmanship, and aesthetic sensibilities can adapt to changing consumer lifestyles. While warm neutrals offer a retreat into natural calm, heritage-inspired patterns connect past and present, and velvet-driven elegance bridges comfort with function. Each

theme responds to the evolving expectation that interiors must not only look elevated but also support everyday living with durability and adaptability.

At the heart of TWOPAGES's seasonal outlook lies a consistent brand philosophy. Rooted in simplicity, a neutral lifestyle, and uncompromising quality, the brand seeks to fuse artistry with function. The result is a portfolio of products that transform homes into spaces where texture, beauty, and comfort coexist. In the 2025 Autumn/Winter season, TWOPAGES once again demonstrates that luxury in interiors is no longer about excess, but about creating an atmosphere where aesthetic refinement and practical performance work hand in hand.

About TWOPAGES Curtains

Founded in 2015 with a mission to revolutionize the window treatment industry, TWOPAGES Curtains has become a leader in providing high-quality, customizable solutions that simplify the shopping experience. With a focus on transparency, innovation, and customer satisfaction, TWOPAGES continues to set the standard for excellence in the industry.

Stay updated with TWOPAGES' latest information, please visit:

Website: https://twopagescurtains.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/twopagescurtains/

SOURCE TWOPAGES
